solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1533E - Chess Team Forming
Let's begin with solving the task for a single option in any complexity that's better than trying all permutations.
Let the members of the opposing team be sorted in a non-decreasing order. Consider some answer — a permutation of members of Polycarp's team.
It's claimed that the members of Polycarp's team also being sorted in a non-decreasing order is one of the optimal answers. Consider a permutation that isn't sorted. Look at the first inversion. So there is some position $$$i$$$ such that $$$a_i  \gt  a_{i+1}$$$, $$$b_i  \lt  b_{i+1}$$$, the contribution to the answer is $$$max(b_i - a_i, b_{i+1} - a_{i+1})$$$. However, $$$b_i - a_i$$$ is not smaller than $$$b_i - a_{i+1}$$$ and $$$b_{i+1} - a_{i+1}$$$ is not smaller than $$$b_{i+1} - a_i$$$. Thus, swapping $$$a_i$$$ and $$$a_{i+1}$$$ won't make the answer any larger. Continue swapping inversions until none are left.
That allows us to produce a solution in $$$O(n)$$$ per query: sort the members of Polycarp's and the opposing teams beforehand, find where to insert the new member and calculate the maximum in linear time.
Now observe the resulting pairs. For some prefix of the pairs, the matched ones are $$$a_i$$$ and $$$b_i$$$ after the sorting. For some suffix, they move no more than one position and matches $$$a_i$$$ with $$$b_{i+1}$$$. And there's an extra pair that contains an option player.
Thus, it's possible to prepare the prefix maximums of $$$b_i - a_i$$$, the suffix maximums of $$$b_{i+1} - a_i$$$, search for the insertion point with a binary search in $$$O(\log n)$$$ and combine the resulting parts in $$$O(1)$$$.
Overall complexity: $$$O((n + m) \log n)$$$.

solutions.content:

toIntArray
()

=

()

toIntArray

a

sort

+

-

()

in
0
until
n

pr
[

+

],

(
